transcript.text: Hello, hello.
How's your day going?
It's pretty good.
How about you?
Yeah, it's great.
I'm in San Francisco this week at the GrowthX office, which is a lot of fun.
And, you know, it's not cold here, but it's cold compared to Houston.
So, wow, I didn't realize Houston gets cold.
Yeah, Houston, you know, not as cold as Dallas, but, you know, it gets a little cold in the wintertime.
Yeah.
What part of Dallas are you in?
I'm near White Rock Lake.
Oh, nice.
Yeah, good spot.
And are you, like, from the Dallas area, grew up there?
Yep, yep.
Grew up in Plano, went to TCU, moved to New York, and then was there for four years, moved back about a year ago.
Okay, nice.
What brought you back home from New York?
I just needed a break.
I wasn't actually intending on, like, fully moving back, but after I came back and started seeing family regularly and was, like, spending more time in nature.
And seeing friends from school, I like, maybe I should move back.
Yeah, for sure.
Yeah, I was, after college at A&M, I went to SF for three years.
And then I moved to New York, like the year before COVID.
And I was convinced I was going to be in New York for the long haul.
But then COVID hit, I came back home.
I met my now fiance.
And when I met her, it was funny, we were going on some dates.
And I was like, hey, I just want to let you know, like, I really think I'm going to be moving back to New York in a couple of months.
And then that never happened.
And so, you know, you can never fully leave Texas.
There's always something pulling you back there, right?
Yeah, I know all to all.
Perfect.
Well, excited to chat with you today and really just like get to know you better.
And would love, you know, for you to pick my brain about how things are going on the sales side here at GrowthX.
I've got some questions that I've got to ask.
But I think like the first thing I just wanted to.
I a little bit better is, you know, like tell me a little bit about in your career, you've like been at most places for like a year and you've done a lot of like really cool, interesting things.
But I guess like tell me about that story of like why you've been so many places and I guess like why none of the stops have been like much longer than a year.
And then also like I'd love to learn a little bit about what you're doing now because it sounds like you're really like your own boss right now, which is super cool.
Yeah, so at first, when I first got into SaaS at Picon AI, I realized that I needed a path for to grow into leadership and there wasn't that path there at the time.
So I left and went to Sorbet where I was head of sales and Sorbet was a great company.
I got to do a lot of things and build a lot of things, but at the end of the day, the product.
Didn't have product market fit and wasn't viable.
So I thought I should kind of take a pause on trying to grow a sales org and really get back to basics.
And I left Pecan in a better state than I found it.
So I thought that's really where I want to go back.
And they had grown a lot too.
They had hired a VP of sales and built out their sales team and brought on RevOps and grew marketing.
So they were crushing it.
So I was super excited about the opportunity or potential.
I was hoping like that was an option.
So I reached out to the CEO and said, you know, I'm interested in coming back if the door is still open.
And he was like, it's open for sure.
He's like, but I'm not, you running sales anymore.
We have a VP of sales.
So the best I can do is make you a nice introduction and, you know, you'll have to take it from there.
So I went back to Pecan and I did get a warning from another team member before coming back saying, these are not.
As great as they look on the outside, and he shared some of his concerns, and I was like, it doesn't really scare me, like the startup world, been there before.

So I went back, and very quickly, I realized things were much worse, like they lost a big client right out of the gate, layoffs happened, laid off a third of the company, and then a few months later, laid off even more people.
And there was more issues around product market fit again, and like building pipeline was a challenge, we weren't getting support.
So I regretted it, and wish that I hadn't gone back and gone somewhere else, but was able to find another position at a company called Houseo, which is technical, synthetic data sales.

That was, again, another challenge, trying, they wanted to sell to enterprises as a very young company that had some good reputable logos, but they didn't really know how to sell into, they didn't know how to break into enterprises and sell.
I was trying to build that out, but wasn't getting the support I needed.
The person I reported to was a lawyer, had no go-to-market experience, couldn't develop me, didn't really see the value and the tools and the support I was asking for.
I just thought like, well, we can lean on our CEO's Rolodex and we can get in the door that way.
So I think it was just seeing like a novel idea around some of these startups is what attracted me.
And then once I get in the door, it's like, you know, I was sold something different than what reality is.
And now, you know, I have to pick it up and run with it.
And sometimes you just can't, you can't be successful.
So I decided I have to cut bait and move on.
So from there, I went to OmniKey and OmniKey was great in the beginning.
The woman that hired me came from Google.
She had been in Google for like almost 18 years and had a wealth of knowledge.
And I thought I could learn a ton from her.
And I got in, did really well, like, and it was difficult at the time because the company was shifting from being more services-oriented to being more truly SaaS.
And so we had to, like, redo the story.
I found a huge opportunity in building out a custom demo process.
It kind of seems like a silly idea, but I noticed that the way that they had been telling the story of the demo wasn't very sticky and people just weren't getting it.

So I said, why don't we just take a brand's assets, load it into the OmniKey platform, and then make creative for them and then tie everything back into how this will optimize their creative process, how we can use the performance data from the ads that they're running to actually close the loop on increasing the creative performance and then accelerating, like, you know, more clicks, more conversions.
A better return on real estate, and so on.
So that small tweak ended up helping me close several deals, even customers that didn't have to go through a pilot process.
So I found success there.
But then a few months later, my boss came to me and said, I've resigned.
She said, if I were you, I would, because you're going to be promoted, congratulations, but if I were you, would start looking for a new position.
She was the third sales leader to leave within a year.
Also, the CS team had walked out.
So there was just a lot of chaos kind of all bubbling up at the top that I had been shielded from.
And then after being promoted, saw it firsthand.
Yeah, absolutely.
Because, like, I get it, right?

In sales, like, you want to go where there's opportunity, and you don't want to just, like, stay at somewhere for two years plus, three years plus, just so you can, like, talk about loyalty, right, in your resume or, like, tenure.
But at the same time, it's like, for this hire at GrowthX, I think we really need someone who is invested for the long haul, right?
Like the opportunity is really here compared to a lot of early stage situations.
And the problem that I have right now, it's not like closing deals.
It's like choosing the right customers, working cross-functionally with the delivery org to figure out how can we make sprints more scalable.
As we're moving from services to SaaS ourselves, it's like, okay, well, what's that launch plan look like?
And how can we use this product to sign all these customers who maybe weren't the best ICP fit?
And so what I'm hearing from you is like, you know, it was like career-oriented, right?
Like you're in sales, you wanted to find ways to advance your career.
But also, you just weren't at companies where you saw a lot of growth, where you saw products that were really differentiated and were going to, you know, be the leaders in their categories.
And so...
so you kind of like took it on yourself to find opportunities that could be steps up, right?
As opposed to staying somewhere like where you didn't have that confidence.
Am I, I don't want to like speak for you, but is that, is that accurate?
Would you change any, any part of what I'm saying?
I think you're right.
I think the, the other thing too, is that these, a lot of these companies were just not very stable either.
So, you know, they promised customers one thing, but then when you look at what is actually being produced and delivered, it's something else.
But we, so it made, it made it very difficult to close deals.

And also what I'm, what I'm looking for next is something that I can like really accelerate in, like pick up and run with and solve some of those early stage problems and, and close deals at a faster clip.
But yeah, I think for the most part, like what you said, summed it up.
Makes sense.
And I guess, what are you doing right now?
Are you doing like some freelance work?
And you're basically working with different clients that you sign on who need help with clay and like outbound orchestration?
Or tell me more about that.
Yeah.
So during the career, during my short career break, or what I've been doing has been dedicated to learning clay and really creating, like really understanding what clay can do for B2B sales teams.
So I'm still definitely learning it, but have one customer now in this, she would be easy to hand off.
I have tons of other connections that would be more than happy to work with her.
But yeah, it's been learning clay, learning how to use clay to pull in data from, you know, whatever data source you want, manipulate it, right?
Personalized emails that you can push out to instantly, smart lead, map TAMs for various.
And help them under like hone in on an ICP and then use different signals within Clay, like buyer intent or job openings or postings and things like that.
Move those into automated workflows that trigger different events that can send out some sort of cold message or post or something.
Super cool.
Yeah, I saw some of your LinkedIn posts talking about the use cases and makes the salesperson's life a lot easier.
If they can figure out how to use a tool like that to automate, you know, the very heavy manual work.
I guess what made you want to choose that tool in particular and like start the podcast as well?
The podcast was more of a hobby.
When I was at TCU, I worked at the TCU radio station and then I worked at CBS radio as my first job ever.
And so I kind of missed like that world a little bit and thought that starting a podcast could be a fun way to connect with people, build relationships.
relationships, and learn.
So I was able to have some fun conversations.
I've kind of put that on hold.
It was more of a heavy lift than I thought it would be.
And then also like the behind the scenes editing, I can't stand.
So yeah, I put that on pause.
But choosing Clay, Clay is like, compared to what Salesforce used to be, like, it's this huge, robust thing.
And there's so many different things that you can do with it.
And people don't really understand it.
So there's a huge opportunity there.
Yeah.
So I really just saw it as something to do in the meantime, until I can find my next position.
But yeah, it's a fantastic tool.
And I think it would be like nice added value and a nice skill set I could bring into my next company.
Makes total sense.
Like, there's probably not a better tool than Clay than you could choose that could be like applicable to client work.
Other
But then implementing a CRM and getting Salesforce or HubSpot set up, but then you'd be a RevOps person, not a salesperson.
Can you think of another tool besides Clay that has that much power for sales and go-to-market teams?
Clay's probably the biggest one, I think.
Ample Market is another good one I've come across recently.
Have you checked them out?
I've definitely heard of them.
Is it more like account intel and lead scoring?
It's like all of the above, but it's different than Clay.
Clay, you really need to get your hands dirty.
Ample Market, you can automate more of the work for you.
And then it also has a big marketing component.
So it's like if you're a team of one or two, it can do tasks to make you seem like you're a team of 10.
Right, right.
And Nooks is like incredible for just...
The cold calling world we're in today.
So there's a lot of cool sales tools out there.
One thing I just want to understand from you better, because it's a huge need here that I'm now just getting into a place where I'm starting to make progress on it.
And I think like with a second person joining, there'll be this opportunity for us to collaborate in a really interesting way for like two account executives, right?
Because like the challenge won't be closing deals here.
It will be closing the right deals and then also building the processes that will make everything more scalable, but like doing it in like a cross-functional way, right?
Like not just our work, but like our work plus delivery.
And so, you you talk a lot about like sales playbooks that you've built at different stops in your career.
Outside of the outbound side, since we don't need as much help there, just tell me about like one of the sales playbooks that you built at one of these companies and how you like approached.
Like.
Building a playbook when there wasn't any guide, right, for you to go to in terms of the sales process.
Not in terms of, like, generating pipeline, in terms of the sales process specifically.

I'd love to just, like, hear your thought process about, like, how you did that, how you went about it, like, what that playbook ended up being outside of pipeline generation, since we don't need that.
Let me think for a minute.
That's been such, like, pipeline generation has been, like, such a focus for the last couple of years.
Yeah, and it's a weird thing, right?
Like, we don't need help generating pipeline.
You're going to have more pipeline here than you could ever possibly hope for, which is, like, the best thing ever as a salesperson, right?
Yeah.
But we just have to figure out, like, how can we be more scalable?
How can we choose the right clients?
How can we have a sales process that really qualifies these clients, right?
Yeah.
The most, there's two things that come to mind.
And I wouldn't really call them playbooks, but more...
when how you
Processes we created.
When I was at HowSo, because it was such a technical solution, I worked very closely with a sales engineer, and we would often have to pull in executive leadership to help on demos.
So we redid the sales process and then documented it to kind of choreograph how we want each sales process.
This is what our best sales process looks like.
We executed it and documented each step of the way.

So we started with discovery, then had another, like what we call deep dive discovery, where we pulled in other stakeholders that hold information relevant to the use case that we were trying to solve and laid out specific metrics that we need to uncover in this meeting to really make the use case viable so that we can take those metrics and tie our, like tie back our solution as to how we will help them hit the metrics or exceed what they're.
After that meeting, we came up with like a choreographed demo where we basically scripted out every part of the demo for each person that was speaking.
The sales engineer, you know, we went through practice rounds too.
did like two practice rounds where the sales engineer pitched everything.
We gave feedback.
And then when we actually went to the big presentation where we presented our solution and demo to all of the stakeholders and the company CEO, like we had it down tight.

And then we also had a return on investment summary built out really nicely that the rest of our team verified, like based on the information that they've had, based on the recordings of the calls that we run with them.
We were like, do you think this is accurate?
Like, will we be actually saving them this much time, this much money, and will this be the return on investment?
And so we kind of standardized that and turn that into.
And then ran that through every future sales process that we had, just like that.
Did that answer your question?
Yeah, definitely.
That's a good example.
With the demo, like, what were the demos that they were giving prior to putting this in place?
Like, was there anything that you could draw from or, like, leverage as the foundation?
There were some demos that were, they were super technical.
Like, they were, they stirred away from, like, the business value.
was getting too into the weeds on, like, the types of models that, like, the types of neural net models that were being used under the hood in the platform.
And when you, when I went back and looked at the calls, I was like, these people aren't data scientists.
Like, they don't really care about this.
They don't know what you're talking about.
And you could see that they were, like, you were losing them on the call.
So, we use that as, like, this is what not to do.
is do.
do.
Let's everything that we're talking about, like stay away from the technical stuff.
They don't care.
Like, let's all tie it back to business value.
Like, what are they going to get out of this?
And I think taking that mindset and looking at future demos through that lens made a big difference.
Makes a lot of sense.
And so you worked with the, like, solutions engineer, the more technical person.
Who else did you work with in, like, creating some of these deliverables, the ROI summary, the deck?
Did you do that all yourself?
Or, like, were there other people involved in that process?
No, I would take, like, the first pass at it and then send it out to other people for feedback.
But it was the chief product officer.
It was our, my boss, who I said was the lawyer.
Even though she had no sales experience, she was very good at storytelling and presentation.
So she was involved and she made a very good deck as well.
So CPO, her title, I forgot her title, actually, what it was, but.
Her, and then the sales engineer, and then we had a head of engineering, and then the chief technical officer, who was also one of the co-founders.
This was usually like my sales support team.
Right, right.
And how fast did that happen, right?
Like, I'm sure you get somewhere, and then you see all these things that are wrong with the sales process, or that can be improved upon.
So, just walk me through, like, the timeline of, like, when you started making those improvements to the sales process, and, like, when you guys got to a finished state.
Yeah, so, it was pretty quick.
Like, once I understood where the company was in their sales process, like, in the first 30 days, then started taking inbound calls, and trying to reframe everything from there.
That particular sales process we ran, I think, was around, like, three months or so.
So, each step was iterative, and the process was a bit fluid, but the whole...
And like new sales process was accomplished in about three months during that time.
Nice, nice.
Pretty quick.
It sounds like some of your approaches, you know, you're selling, right?
Like you're figuring out how to sell the product.

But as you're doing that, you're also sort of taking a step back, thinking strategically and like finding ways to build process, like finding ways to make things scalable, to improve on like the way it's currently being done.
And you find a way to like do those two things in parallel.
Yeah, it's not easy.
But whenever I do something, I tend to think like, was that the best way we could have done it?
Could we do it better?
Even if like we nailed it, I'm always like, yeah, but could we do it a little bit better next time?
Or how can we create efficiencies or get closer to like the ideal outcome that we want each and every time?
Yeah, I mean, I'm personally really excited to just have someone to collaborate with on a lot of this.
It does feel like I'm thinking about...
in a vacuum, right?
Like there are people who will lead the eight-week strategy sprint, which is phase one, but that person has changed, right?
And now it's going back to someone who had done a lot of that work because we're trying to hire to have someone sort of take that on full-time.
And we're collaborating closely here, but it's just like, you know, everything that I'm learning, I'm learning on my own, right?
Like I'm learning for the first time.

And so I think I'm like eager to collaborate with someone that can just quickly understand a lot of what I've been working through and then work together with me on like finding ways to make it more of a scalable process, more of something where like when the next salesperson comes on board, they're going to have a playbook to work from, you know?
The playbook right now is pretty much just in my head.
So you'd be like onboarded well, but like we would have to build this together, right?
Like as, as you're here.
Okay.
Some of the questions that I have to ask you so that every interview has.
Some objectivity to it.
Tell me about at any of, you know, the stops in your career, like how did you position the product's value to a marketing leader as part of like a deal that you closed?
Like, just help me kind of understand how you position the product's value to someone that is a marketing decision maker.
Yeah.
So thinking back at OmniKey, pretty much every deal was always tied to helping them achieve their North Star metric.
So as part of discovery, we're trying to understand where are you today?
Like, what is your return on ad spend?
And it's from a general perspective, but also, and let me say, return on ad spend is usually the metric that people care most about.
Occasionally it's, it's CAC or if it's, um, if they're not looking for like direct response, it's click-through rate in some cases, but usually ROAS is the most important thing.
So it's understanding at a holistic level, what is their ROAS and
And then at a ad platform level, and then if we break it down per campaign, and then how much time is their team spending on creative generation, ideation, reviews?
Like, what does that process look like between the time that they're like, shoot, this creative is fatigued, we need to replace it.
And then how long does it take for them to actually get new creative out?
So understanding where they are and where they would like to go, and then filling in the gaps on how OmniKey can get them there.
So one of the biggest benefits of OmniKey is that you use AI to review your campaign performance, and then that campaign performance data is plugged into creative generation.

So if, for example, your logo, or not your logo, your creative does really well when the logo is like, of a certain size and a certain placement, or you use this call to action or this color scheme, this works better than this other color scheme.
And you can create those ads in every size you need for all of your meta campaigns in a few minutes.
That's a huge time savings.
And then we forecast the potential return on ad spend or whatever key metrics they're interested in hitting.
Telling the story around helping them hit those metrics, time savings, cost savings, and then also understanding why.
Like getting deep into like, why do you care about Realize?
It might seem like a silly question, but maybe they've done really well.
Maybe Realize, maybe they've been doing really well in Realize Dipped recently, or maybe they're looking to like hit a certain revenue mark.
Really trying to understand like what's the larger business objective behind that, behind achieving that Northstar KPI.
Makes sense.
And I guess why would OmniKey like be a better solution than alternatives out there?
And maybe it's like, you know, not completely.
And that's part of the reason why, you know.
You decided to leave, but what made it the right choice versus other options?
I think, well, initially, I think it was that they were the only company in the space.

If you look at other companies that use AI to make creative, OmniKey seems to be the only company that really leans on data, although that was the part that was not quite right, but that was a part of the story.

And we did have a dashboard and things that we could demo as a part of that, but I think that was a key differentiator, and I think that was that light bulb moment for people that was like, oh, this isn't just based on an open AI model that is trained on everyone's data.
This is trained on my data.
So I think that's what got people excited and ultimately converted them.
I love that.
Yeah, there's always a story.
And we definitely have a good one here, and a lot of it all connects back to Marcel.
Which is the reason why there's no needs on the outbound side.
Okay, connected to this, tell me about one of the biggest deals you closed or one of the deals you're most proud of.
Like, how did you close it?
You know, I have a comment in every sales interview.
You got to ask this question, right?
Yeah.
I mean, biggest and most proud are two separate stories at, like, different ends of the spectrum.
Most proud is very early in my career when I worked at an agency.
I would go out with, it was the company that owns the Dallas Morning News.

So I was, like, the digital marketing expert that would go out with newspaper sales reps and talk to all of their accounts and try to convert them from, like, running newspaper ads into pay-per-click or target or content creation, display media, whatever we thought we could sell.
And there was a new...
Paper rep that had a local furniture company.
There were like four stores around DFW.
And she was like, I think we can sell them pay-per-click.
She's like, but I don't know anything about it.
You know, I just want you to come speak to him.
And I was like, okay, cool.
What can I do?
I was like, what do you think I can do to like relate to him?
Because I've was, this is fairly early in my career.
I really didn't know what I was doing yet.
And she goes, I think you should go visit one of his stores.
So one night I drove out to one of his stores.
And then as I was driving, the idea came to me that I should take pictures of all of the different, take pictures of some of the different items in the store.
And then the description, like the specific item, the like size of it, the coloring, whatever.
So I found like some bar stools.
I found a really high end dining room table.
And then I went back home, got on Google AdWords and pulled the search queries for all of these keywords verbatim off of the tags in the store.

And then put a radius, like a 10-mile radius around each of his locations and said, look, within 10 miles at each of your stores for items that are on your floor, these are how many searches are coming up per day.
And guess how many times you come up?
Zero.
Guess how many times your competitors come up?
And then I showed all the competitors and he was like, and then the deal closed pretty quick.
It was like a week later he signed.
So, yeah, I was like, I did a really good job of creating fear of, or using loss aversion for that deal and creating Fathom.
Yeah, for sure.
And it sounds like you had to like learn how to maybe use a tool you hadn't used before to like dig into the search queries.
Is that true?
Is that like part of the process you had to go through there?
Yeah, it wasn't typical.
I had maybe signed into AdWords two times before that, but it was fairly intuitive to use.
So I was able to find that information.
And take screenshots and then add it to a presentation.
Yeah, it makes a lot of sense.
It's a great example.
What about a deal that is like more recent that was one that's like a traditional kind of software deal?
Tell me like a little bit about the story behind how you closed that deal.
Yeah, at OmniKey, there was a customer, a pest control company, which is a little bit outside of OmniKey's wheelhouse.
But they, I think they saw an ad and they came in and they thought, this could be interesting.
I want to learn more about like what you're doing to run ads because we're trying to get more leads around commercial pest control.
Like we want to go after, you know, office buildings, apartment complexes, stuff like that.
And we're kind of having issues getting those meetings.
So, you know, what can you do?
And I said, well, and he also like was a kind of a technical like IT guy, but he was in charge of marketing.
So he didn't really.
It wasn't.
He really understood what I was saying initially.
So I really had to tell the story in a different way, kind of relate it to something that was worth speaking his language in IT terms.
But I basically told the story around the data.

We will take your existing campaign performance data, we will ingest it, we'll use AI to analyze it, we'll analyze all of the creative that you're running, and then we'll be able to surface the different elements of the creative that is performing better.

So like it could be this color or this picture of an apartment complex, or it could be like someone with a, it could be an image of someone wearing the pest control's uniform standing in front of a service truck.
And so we'll be able to identify those key elements, surface them, and then recombine them into like your next set of winning ads to help drive in those conversions.
And he was like, okay, this is interesting.
Then he sent me an RFI.
Um, so filled that out.
Yeah.
Your favorite.
Right.
Uh, so filled that out.
Then he was like, well, we do have one marketing person.
She's more on the content side.
She's kind of a jack of all trades.
Um, so I'll bring her in.
She didn't really understand what OmniKey was either.
She asked a lot of questions, but like had trouble even getting the questions out, like couldn't articulate what she was trying to ask very well.
So I'd like translate it.
And I think in that process, cause I'd always be like, I know what you're saying.
Are you saying this?
And she was like, yes.
And then we built trust and a nice rapport.
And I did this completely solo.
had been OmniKey for like 60 days at this point.
And at this point, the company was used to, um, starting every, starting new customers off on a three month pilot, usually around like $2,500 a month.
And going through this process, I got them to sign an annual contract for $5,000 a month without a pilot, completely solo.
Nice.
Nice.
Love that.
Love avoiding a pilot.
Definitely have sold pilots in my career.
And our phase one is like a pilot to some extent.
And would love to find ways that we can just get right into phase two.
And probably we could do that by just doing a better job, deeper job in the sales process and building the ability to take a piece of content that they've created and refresh it using our workflows.
Right.
Or like take concepts or take a keyword that they want to go after and then build a net new piece of content using our workflows.
These are things that like I know would be helpful, but that we just haven't had time to be able to make progress on.
So, you know, the ideas are there.
It's just like the execution really that is what needs to happen.
With that example at OmniKey, like what do you feel like was your biggest personal accomplishment?
And then also like what do you feel like was your biggest failure or like thing you could have done better?
So I think my biggest personal accomplishment was that I got the deal closed without a pilot for an annual term for sure.
And then I think what I could have done better was we didn't onboard them immediately.
